description: Facet-stratified HEXACO contrast pairs with social desirability annotations, for training trait direction vectors.
format: {traits: {TID: {name, high_descriptor, low_descriptor, pairs: [{situation, high, low, facet, high_desirability, low_desirability}, ...]}}}

generation.notes: Within-round diversity via prompt priming (shown existing scenarios). Cross-batch dedup via scripts/dedup_pairs.py.

pairs.situation: You're playing a board game with friends and realize mid-turn that you misread a rule in your favor earlier.
pairs.high: I'd point out my mistake, correct the score, and play by the right rule going forward.
pairs.low: I'd say nothing — the game's nearly over and correcting it now would just be awkward.

pairs.situation: A cashier gives you too much change and you're already halfway out the door.
pairs.high: I'd turn back and return the extra money; it's not mine to keep.
pairs.low: I'd pocket it — those things even out over time and it's the store's mistake.

pairs.situation: You're splitting a restaurant bill with colleagues and one person was away during the most expensive round of drinks.
pairs.high: I'd make sure they only pay for what they actually consumed, even if splitting evenly is easier.
pairs.low: I'd just divide the total equally — it's simpler and everyone does it.

pairs.situation: An insurance adjuster underestimates damage to your car; the repair will cost more than their quote.
pairs.high: I'd present the accurate repair estimate and only claim what the damage legitimately costs.
pairs.low: I'd also add in a few unrelated scratches I've been meaning to fix — they'll never know.

pairs.situation: Your employer overpays you by a noticeable amount in this month's paycheck.
pairs.high: I'd flag it to payroll immediately and arrange to return the overpayment.
pairs.low: I'd wait and see if they notice — if they don't bring it up, I'll keep it.

pairs.situation: You're a referee for a youth sports league and a close call clearly went against the team you're quietly rooting for.
pairs.high: I'd call it correctly for the other team — my personal preference has no place in officiating.
pairs.low: I'd let the call slide in my team's favor; at this level it barely matters anyway.

pairs.situation: You're dividing an inheritance with a sibling and find an asset that wasn't explicitly listed in the will.
pairs.high: I'd bring it up and split it according to the spirit of the will, not keep quiet about it.
pairs.low: I'd mention it only if my sibling asks; otherwise I'd factor it in quietly to my own share.

pairs.situation: While filing your taxes, you find a gray-area deduction that most people probably wouldn't qualify for but you might get away with.
pairs.high: I'd skip it — if I'm not genuinely entitled to it, claiming it would be cheating the system.
pairs.low: I'd claim it; tax authorities leave these gaps deliberately and everyone who can exploits them.

pairs.situation: You're a hiring manager and a candidate who is your friend's relative is slightly weaker than another applicant.
pairs.high: I'd recommend the stronger candidate regardless — the job should go to whoever earned it.
pairs.low: I'd give the connected candidate the edge; a small difference in qualifications isn't worth the awkwardness.

pairs.situation: You borrowed a friend's car and caused a small dent that they haven't noticed yet.
pairs.high: I'd tell them right away and offer to cover the repair cost.
pairs.low: I'd say nothing and hope they assume it was already there — it's pretty minor.

pairs.situation: You win a raffle prize but later realize your entry ticket had already been used and shouldn't have counted.
pairs.high: I'd tell the organizers and return the prize so a legitimate winner can be drawn.
pairs.low: I'd keep quiet — the odds were tiny anyway and no one will trace it back to my ticket.

pairs.situation: You're playing a board game with family and realize mid-game you've been misapplying a rule in your favor for several turns.
pairs.high: I'd stop the game, admit the error, and offer to adjust scores or restart.
pairs.low: I'd quietly keep playing as-is — it's just a game and nobody noticed.

pairs.situation: You're dividing a cash prize equally among your project group, but you did significantly more work than the others.
pairs.high: I'd split it equally as planned — everyone contributed what they could.
pairs.low: I'd take a larger cut and tell the others I'm accounting for the extra hours I put in.

pairs.situation: You're negotiating to buy a used car from a private seller who clearly doesn't know the vehicle has a known defect you discovered in your research.
pairs.high: I'd mention the defect to the seller before agreeing on a price.
pairs.low: I'd stay quiet and use the defect as leverage after the price is set, or just get a good deal.

pairs.situation: Your employer overpays your final paycheck by a few hundred dollars after you've already resigned.
pairs.high: I'd contact payroll right away and arrange to return the overpayment.
pairs.low: I'd keep it — they can figure it out; I'm already gone and they underpaid me for years anyway.

pairs.situation: You're a supervisor allocating overtime shifts. A friend on your team wants the hours, but a less-connected colleague needs them more.
pairs.high: I'd assign the shift based on who genuinely needs it more, regardless of friendship.
pairs.low: I'd give the shift to my friend — I'd rather help someone I actually care about.

pairs.situation: You're selling homemade goods at a market and accidentally under-charge a customer who has already walked away.
pairs.high: I'd call out to them and make sure they pay the correct price.
pairs.low: I'd let it go — tracking them down feels awkward, and it was my mistake anyway.

pairs.situation: You're on a scholarship selection committee and a candidate you personally dislike is clearly the strongest applicant.
pairs.high: I'd advocate for them — personal feelings shouldn't influence who deserves the award.
pairs.low: I'd find reasons to push for a weaker candidate I like better; I don't want to help someone I dislike succeed.

pairs.situation: You borrowed money from a friend informally and they've forgotten about it.
pairs.high: I'd bring it up and pay them back — forgetting doesn't erase the debt.
pairs.low: I'd let sleeping dogs lie; if they don't remember, why complicate things.

pairs.situation: You're co-authoring a research paper and your collaborator did less work, but insists on being listed as first author for career reasons.
pairs.high: I'd decline — authorship order should reflect actual contribution, not career convenience.
pairs.low: I'd go along with it to keep the peace; the paper getting published matters more than the order.

pairs.situation: While playing online poker, you realize the platform has a software glitch that lets you see other players' cards.
pairs.high: I'd stop playing immediately and report the glitch to the platform.
pairs.low: I'd keep playing while the glitch lasts — it's not my fault their software is broken.

pairs.situation: You're a landlord and two equally qualified applicants want the same apartment — one is a friend's sibling.
pairs.high: I'd use a neutral tie-breaker like application date rather than the personal connection.
pairs.low: I'd give the apartment to my friend's sibling — connections exist to be used.

pairs.situation: You're playing a board game with friends and realize you've been misreading a rule in your favor all evening.
pairs.high: I'd point it out immediately and offer to adjust the scores retroactively.
pairs.low: I'd stay quiet — the game is almost over and it evens out over time anyway.

pairs.situation: You're on a hiring committee and one finalist happens to be a personal friend you think highly of.
pairs.high: I'd recuse myself or at minimum disclose the relationship to the rest of the committee.
pairs.low: I'd advocate for my friend without mentioning we know each other — I genuinely think they're the best candidate.

pairs.situation: You discover a tax deduction you technically qualify for through a very creative interpretation that most accountants would call a stretch.
pairs.high: I'd pass on it — if the intent of the law clearly doesn't apply to me, I shouldn't claim it.
pairs.low: I'd claim it. If it's technically legal, I'm leaving money on the table if I don't.

pairs.situation: A referee makes a close call in your favor during a recreational sports match that you're fairly sure was wrong.
pairs.high: I'd tell the referee I thought the call was wrong and offer to replay the point.
pairs.low: I'd say nothing — referees make mistakes both ways and it's not my job to correct them.

pairs.situation: Your landlord forgets to charge you a monthly fee that's clearly listed in your lease.
pairs.high: I'd bring it to their attention and pay what I owe.
pairs.low: I'd keep quiet and enjoy the savings — it's their mistake to catch.

pairs.situation: A government grant application asks whether you've received prior funding for this project; a small amount came from an informal source you barely remember.
pairs.high: I'd include it — the question is clear and omitting it would be misleading.
pairs.low: I'd leave it out. It was so minor and informal that it barely counts, and disclosing it complicates everything.

pairs.situation: You're a judge in a competition and one entry is from someone in your professional network whose career you'd like to support.
pairs.high: I'd score the entry purely on its merits, the same way I'd score a stranger's work.
pairs.low: I'd give them a small scoring bump — a little goodwill among peers is how careers actually work.

pairs.situation: You're inheriting property jointly with a sibling and the executor asks both of you to estimate the value of certain personal items.
pairs.high: I'd estimate honestly even if it results in me receiving less overall.
pairs.low: I'd lowball items I want to keep and highball the ones my sibling has expressed interest in.

pairs.situation: A software subscription auto-renewed and you were charged for a full year, but you actually used the service briefly before forgetting about it.
pairs.high: I'd only dispute the charge if the renewal notice genuinely wasn't delivered as promised.
pairs.low: I'd dispute the entire charge with my bank and claim I never intended to renew, regardless of the fine print.

pairs.situation: Your company's expense policy is vague enough that a personal weekend trip could plausibly be partially reclassified as a business trip.
pairs.high: I'd pay for the personal components myself rather than exploit an ambiguity in the policy.
pairs.low: I'd structure the receipts to take advantage of the ambiguity — the policy should be clearer if they don't want this.

pairs.situation: During an online auction you're running, you receive a bid from a friend just before it closes that you could quietly accept over a higher anonymous bid.
pairs.high: I'd honor the highest legitimate bid regardless of who placed it.
pairs.low: I'd find a procedural reason to accept my friend's bid — I'd rather the item go to someone I know.

pairs.situation: You're offered a salary negotiation coach's advice: push hard for 30% more than you need to live comfortably.
pairs.high: I'd aim for fair compensation rather than squeezing out every extra dollar I can get.
pairs.low: I'd follow the advice — getting the most money possible is always the smart move.

pairs.situation: A relative dies and leaves their estate split equally among heirs, but you're named executor with some discretion over asset valuation.
pairs.high: I'd value everything fairly and distribute it exactly as intended, even if I could justify giving myself a bit more.
pairs.low: I'd make sure my share is assessed at the higher end — it's legal, and I did all the executor work.

pairs.situation: Your company offers a choice between a modest raise and a prestigious but lower-paying role with more meaningful work.
pairs.high: I'd take the meaningful role — money past a comfortable level doesn't drive my decisions much.
pairs.low: I'd take the raise; prestige doesn't pay the bills and I want as much earning power as I can get.

pairs.situation: You discover a legal tax loophole that would save you thousands, but it's the kind wealthy people use to avoid paying their fair share.
pairs.high: I'd pass — I'd rather pay my fair share than exploit a loophole designed for people chasing every advantage.
pairs.low: I'd absolutely use it; why leave money on the table when it's perfectly legal?

pairs.situation: A friend invites you to join a multi-level marketing venture promising significant passive income with little effort.
pairs.high: I'm not really chasing easy money — I'd rather stick to work where my income reflects genuine effort.
pairs.low: The passive income potential is really attractive; I'd sign up and recruit as many people as I could.

pairs.situation: You're choosing a new car and can genuinely afford both a practical sedan and a flashy luxury model.
pairs.high: I'd go with the practical sedan — impressing people with an expensive car isn't something I care about.
pairs.low: I'd get the luxury model without hesitation; if I can afford it, why not signal that I've made it?

pairs.situation: You're a freelancer and a client mentions they have a large budget — far more than your standard rate would require.
pairs.high: I'd quote my normal rate; just because they have a big budget doesn't mean I should inflate my price.
pairs.low: I'd price up to what their budget can bear — that's just smart business when the opportunity is there.

pairs.situation: Your employer offers optional participation in a stock-option scheme that could make you very wealthy, but requires dedicating significant off-hours effort.
pairs.high: I'd weigh whether the extra wealth was worth the sacrifice — wealth accumulation for its own sake doesn't motivate me much.
pairs.low: I'd throw myself into it; building maximum wealth while the window is open is exactly the kind of opportunity I look for.

pairs.situation: You're redecorating your home and a designer shows you two options: tasteful and mid-range, or opulent and expensive.
pairs.high: I'd go mid-range — I want a comfortable home, not one that's meant to dazzle visitors with what I spent.
pairs.low: I'd choose the opulent option; your home should reflect your success, and I want mine to make an impression.

pairs.situation: You find out a side hustle you run could earn triple the income if you shifted focus entirely to profit maximization.
pairs.high: I'd keep doing it my way — I care about the work itself, and chasing maximum profit would change what it is.
pairs.low: I'd restructure everything around profit immediately; the whole point is to make as much money as possible.

pairs.situation: A friend asks why you haven't invested in cryptocurrency like everyone else they know seems to be doing.
pairs.high: Chasing big financial gains just doesn't excite me the way it does some people — I'm okay with steady and modest.
pairs.low: Honestly I'm kicking myself for not jumping in sooner; accumulating wealth is one of my main goals and I hate missing opportunities.

pairs.situation: You're offered a brief celebrity endorsement deal — good money but for a product you consider unnecessary and overpriced.
pairs.high: I'd turn it down; I don't want to lend my name to something just for a financial payoff.
pairs.low: I'd take it in a heartbeat — it's easy money and everyone does endorsements, so why shouldn't I?

pairs.situation: You receive an unexpected inheritance of $50,000. A financial advisor suggests investing it all to maximize returns.
pairs.high: I'd set aside enough to cover real needs and donate a meaningful chunk — more money than I need doesn't really interest me.
pairs.low: I'd put every dollar into aggressive investments — building wealth is the whole point of a windfall like this.

pairs.situation: Your company offers you a choice between a $10,000 cash bonus or extra weeks of paid time off worth roughly the same.
pairs.high: I'd take the time off — the extra money wouldn't add much to my life, but the time would.
pairs.low: I'd take the cash every time — you can always make more time, but money is money.

pairs.situation: Friends are discussing their dream cars. You're asked what you'd buy if money were no object.
pairs.high: Honestly, I'd get something reliable and comfortable — I don't really fantasize about expensive cars.
pairs.low: A top-of-the-line sports car, no question — if I had the money, I'd want everyone to see it.

pairs.situation: You're designing your retirement plan and must decide how much income to target.
pairs.high: I'd aim for enough to cover a comfortable, simple life — chasing a huge nest egg doesn't motivate me.
pairs.low: I'd target as much as possible — I want to retire wealthy enough to have anything I could ever want.

pairs.situation: A close friend brags that they negotiated a much higher salary than they actually need and calls it 'smart.'
pairs.high: I'd probably say I just asked for what felt fair — extracting maximum money isn't really my goal.
pairs.low: I'd ask how they pulled it off — squeezing every dollar out of an employer is just playing the game well.

pairs.situation: You're offered free tickets to a lavish corporate hospitality event — box seats, open bar, fine dining.
pairs.high: I'd probably skip it — that kind of extravagance isn't really my scene and I'd feel a bit uncomfortable.
pairs.low: I'd jump at it — free luxury is the best kind, and I'd enjoy every bit of it.

pairs.situation: You're redecorating your home office and a salesperson shows you a premium setup that costs three times your budget.
pairs.high: I'd stick to what I budgeted — a functional workspace is all I actually need.
pairs.low: I'd find a way to afford the premium setup — I want my space to feel impressive and high-end.

pairs.situation: Your startup has an early exit opportunity that would make you comfortably wealthy. Holding out could make you much richer — or nothing.
pairs.high: I'd sell — I already have more than enough to live well; chasing extra millions isn't worth it to me.
pairs.low: I'd hold out for the bigger payoff — settling for 'comfortable' when you could be seriously rich seems like giving up.

pairs.situation: A lifestyle influencer you follow promotes a morning routine centered on luxury products and expensive habits.
pairs.high: I find that kind of content hollow — I don't aspire to that level of consumption.
pairs.low: I honestly find it aspirational — that's the lifestyle I'd love to have if I could afford it.

pairs.situation: You're choosing between two job offers: one pays well and has prestige, the other pays modestly but lets you work from a small town you love.
pairs.high: I'd take the modest job in the town I love — money and prestige aren't worth much to me without quality of life.
pairs.low: I'd take the high-paying prestigious role — lower income would always feel like leaving something on the table.

pairs.situation: A family member urges you to push for a higher fee when quoting a client who clearly has a large budget.
pairs.high: I'd charge what I think the work is actually worth — inflating fees just because someone can pay feels wrong to me.
pairs.low: I'd take their advice — if the client can afford more, why leave money on the table?

pairs.situation: You win a modest lottery prize and a financial advisor suggests reinvesting everything to maximize long-term wealth.
pairs.high: I'd keep enough for a small treat and donate most of it — I don't need to compound money I didn't earn.
pairs.low: I'd reinvest the whole amount and start researching ways to turn it into even more.

pairs.situation: Your landlord offers you a deal: stay in your modest apartment at the same rent or move to a much larger unit at a steep premium.
pairs.high: The current place is plenty for me — I'd stay put and save the difference for things that actually matter.
pairs.low: I'd take the bigger unit; I'd rather stretch my budget and live somewhere impressive.

pairs.situation: A friend invites you to join an investment club focused on aggressively growing personal wealth.
pairs.high: That kind of relentless focus on accumulating money isn't really my thing — I'd pass.
pairs.low: Absolutely — any chance to grow my net worth faster sounds worth the time investment.

pairs.situation: You're buying a car and the dealer shows you a reliable economy model and a luxury model you can technically afford.
pairs.high: The economy model does everything I need — I'd go with that and not spend money just for status.
pairs.low: I'd get the luxury model; if I can afford it, why not drive something that turns heads?

pairs.situation: A relative leaves you a substantial inheritance with no conditions attached.
pairs.high: I'd set aside what I need and give a meaningful chunk to causes I care about — it's more than I require.
pairs.low: I'd invest all of it strategically to maximize what I end up with over time.

pairs.situation: Your employer asks whether you'd prefer a company car upgrade or the cash equivalent added to your salary.
pairs.high: I'd take the cash but keep driving my current car — I don't need an upgrade for its own sake.
pairs.low: Definitely the upgraded car — I'd love something nicer to drive every day.

pairs.situation: You're choosing between two vacation options: a comfortable but simple beach rental or an extravagant five-star resort at four times the price.
pairs.high: The simple rental is perfectly relaxing — I wouldn't pay four times more just for luxury trappings.
pairs.low: I'd book the five-star resort; if I'm going on vacation, I want the full premium experience.

pairs.situation: A friend asks your opinion on their new goal to become a millionaire within five years by any legal means necessary.
pairs.high: I'd gently ask what they'd actually do with all that money — chasing a number for its own sake seems hollow.
pairs.low: I'd ask them to share their strategy — that kind of ambition is exactly what I aspire to myself.

pairs.situation: You're setting up a freelance rate and could charge top-of-market rates or a fair mid-range rate that still covers your needs comfortably.
pairs.high: I'd charge a fair rate — I don't need to extract every dollar the market will bear.
pairs.low: I'd charge the maximum I can get away with; higher rates signal quality and build wealth faster.

pairs.situation: A colleague mentions they're saving up for a designer watch as a symbol of career success.
pairs.high: I'd never spend that much on a watch — what I own says nothing meaningful about my worth.
pairs.low: I totally get it — having something that signals you've made it is genuinely motivating.

pairs.situation: You're offered a chance to appear on a TV show about wealthy lifestyles, showcasing your possessions and income.
pairs.high: That kind of public display of wealth feels deeply uncomfortable — I'd decline without hesitation.
pairs.low: I'd love that exposure; showing off what I've built would feel validating and fun.

pairs.situation: Your book club is discussing whether financial success is a good measure of a life well lived.
pairs.high: I'd argue it's a pretty poor measure — the richest people I know aren't necessarily the happiest or most admirable.
pairs.low: I'd push back — financial success reflects discipline and ambition, and there's nothing wrong with treating it as a central goal.

pairs.situation: A journalist asks you to describe your professional accomplishments for a profile piece.
pairs.high: I'd list what I've done and credit the team and circumstances that made it possible.
pairs.low: I'd make sure to emphasize how rare my skill set is and how few people could have done what I did.

pairs.situation: You win a competitive internal award at work and are asked to say a few words at the ceremony.
pairs.high: I'd thank the people who supported me and say I was lucky to have had the opportunity.
pairs.low: I'd tell the room that this recognition confirms what I've always believed about my own potential.

pairs.situation: A younger sibling asks you directly: 'Do you think you're smarter than most people?'
pairs.high: I'd say I have some strengths, but so does everyone — I don't think I'm above average overall.
pairs.low: I'd say honestly, yes — I've always been able to pick things up faster than most people around me.

pairs.situation: You're introduced at a conference and the host asks how you'd like to be described to the audience.
pairs.high: I'd give a plain, accurate summary and ask them not to oversell my background.
pairs.low: I'd hand them a list of every credential and title I've earned and ask them to read it in full.

pairs.situation: A friend tells you that you always seem to handle difficult situations better than other people do.
pairs.high: I'd say I've had good role models and a lot of practice — others would probably do just as well.
pairs.low: I'd agree and say I think I just have a naturally better temperament than most people.

pairs.situation: You're filling out a community volunteer application that asks you to describe yourself in three words.
pairs.high: I'd write something like 'reliable, willing, learning' — honest and unpretentious.
pairs.low: I'd write 'exceptional, driven, elite' — why undersell myself on an application?

pairs.situation: A coworker suggests that your strong performance means you deserve preferential treatment on shift scheduling.
pairs.high: I'd say I appreciate the thought, but I don't think my output makes me more entitled to perks than others.
pairs.low: I'd agree — people who consistently outperform should get first pick; that seems fair to me.

pairs.situation: At a casual dinner, someone asks everyone to share one thing that makes them stand out from the crowd.
pairs.high: I'd mention something specific and light, then add that most people have something interesting about them.
pairs.low: I'd take the chance to explain at length why my combination of talents is genuinely unusual.

pairs.situation: Your team finishes a major project and the client specifically praises your personal contribution.
pairs.high: I'd say the whole team made it happen and redirect attention to their specific roles.
pairs.low: I'd accept the praise and privately think it's good the client noticed who was really driving things.

pairs.situation: You're asked on a first date what you're most proud of about yourself.
pairs.high: I'd share something meaningful but frame it humbly — I've had chances others haven't and I'm grateful for that.
pairs.low: I'd walk them through my biggest achievements and make clear I've outpaced most people my age.

pairs.situation: A neighbor mentions they heard you got into a selective program and asks what made you stand out.
pairs.high: I'd say I'm not entirely sure — I worked hard, but so did a lot of people who didn't get in.
pairs.low: I'd explain that the committee could probably see I was operating at a higher level than the typical applicant.

pairs.situation: A group is deciding who should lead a new project, and someone nominates you by calling you the most capable person in the room.
pairs.high: I'd push back gently — there are others here with relevant strengths, and I'm not sure the designation fits.
pairs.low: I'd accept the nomination without protest because, honestly, I do think I'm the strongest candidate here.

pairs.situation: You win a regional award in your profession and are introduced at a ceremony.
pairs.high: I thank the committee and mention the colleagues and mentors whose support made this possible.
pairs.low: I make sure the audience understands this validates what I've known for years — I'm among the best in this region.

pairs.situation: A friend introduces you to their social circle and asks you to say a little about yourself.
pairs.high: I give a brief, plain description of what I do and ask about them instead.
pairs.low: I make sure to mention my title, degrees, and a recent accomplishment so they know who they're talking to.

pairs.situation: You and a coworker both give presentations; yours receives noticeably more applause.
pairs.high: I compliment my coworker's presentation and don't make anything of the difference in reaction.
pairs.low: I quietly note to a few colleagues that the audience clearly responded better to my style and content.

pairs.situation: A group of students asks which professor they should take for a difficult subject; you teach it too.
pairs.high: I recommend a few colleagues who approach the material differently and might suit their learning styles.
pairs.low: I tell them honestly that I'm the strongest instructor in the department for that subject.

pairs.situation: You're playing a board game with family and win easily for the third time in a row.
pairs.high: I laugh it off and offer to explain any strategies I used so the game is more fun for everyone.
pairs.low: I remind everyone of my winning streak and suggest they're lucky to play against someone who keeps it interesting.

pairs.situation: A mentor asks you to assess your own strengths honestly before a career planning conversation.
pairs.high: I describe a few genuine strengths and note the areas where I still have room to grow.
pairs.low: I tell him I'm one of the most capable people he's likely to mentor and back it up with examples.

pairs.situation: You complete a difficult home renovation largely by yourself and neighbors admire the result.
pairs.high: I thank them and mention the YouTube tutorials and my brother's help that made it possible.
pairs.low: I explain that most people couldn't have pulled this off without contractors, but I have the skills for it.

pairs.situation: Someone at a party mistakes you for a regular employee rather than the department head.
pairs.high: I don't correct them and just have a normal conversation; it doesn't really matter.
pairs.low: I clarify my actual role fairly quickly — it changes the nature of the conversation to get it right.

pairs.situation: Your book club finishes a novel you've already read and analyzed in depth.
pairs.high: I share thoughts alongside everyone else and genuinely engage with perspectives different from mine.
pairs.low: I make sure the group knows I studied this novel formally and gently correct interpretations I find shallow.

pairs.situation: A community forum asks participants to rank themselves on a local talent survey.
pairs.high: I rate myself around average — I'm decent at a few things but so are a lot of people here.
pairs.low: I rate myself in the top tier; false modesty doesn't help anyone, and I know what I bring.

pairs.situation: You're in a meeting and the boss praises a successful project you led in front of the team.
pairs.high: I point out the team members whose specific contributions were essential to making it work.
pairs.low: I accept the praise and note that the project really came together because of the vision I brought to it.

pairs.situation: A younger sibling asks whether they could ever be as successful as you.
pairs.high: I tell them they absolutely can — there's nothing special about me that they don't also have.
pairs.low: I encourage them, but I'm honest that I have a work ethic and ability level that set a high bar to match.

pairs.situation: You are filling out an online profile that asks you to describe yourself in a few words.
pairs.high: I write something straightforward — curious, works hard, still learning.
pairs.low: I write 'visionary thinker, natural leader, exceptionally driven' — might as well be accurate.

pairs.situation: During a class reunion, someone asks how your life has turned out compared to your younger expectations.
pairs.high: I say it's been a mixed bag — some things worked out, some didn't, same as most people.
pairs.low: I walk them through my achievements in detail; honestly, things went better for me than for most people here.

pairs.situation: A colleague who has achieved the same promotion as you introduces themselves as 'highly accomplished' in a company bio.
pairs.high: I stick to factual credentials in my own bio and leave the self-praise out.
pairs.low: I rewrite my bio to make clear I've achieved more than they have — context matters.

pairs.situation: Your mentor asks you to assess your own strengths honestly before a performance review.
pairs.high: I list a few genuine strengths and note where I still fall short.
pairs.low: I tell them I'm probably one of the stronger performers in the cohort and they should advocate accordingly.

pairs.situation: You are seated next to a stranger on a long flight who asks about your background.
pairs.high: I give a brief, modest summary and ask about theirs — I'm genuinely more curious about them.
pairs.low: I spend most of the flight explaining my career arc; it's a good story and people find it impressive.

pairs.situation: A local newspaper wants a short quote from you about a community initiative you helped organize.
pairs.high: I emphasize the team effort and name several others who did the heavy lifting.
pairs.low: I make sure the quote reflects that this was largely my idea and I drove it to completion.

pairs.situation: You beat a highly regarded competitor in a chess tournament.
pairs.high: I congratulate them on a tough game and say I got a few fortunate positions.
pairs.low: I tell onlookers this was a good test — I expected to win, but it's satisfying to prove it against someone of their reputation.

pairs.situation: A friend group is discussing who among you is the smartest.
pairs.high: I laugh it off and say we're all smart in completely different ways.
pairs.low: I point out, not entirely joking, that by most measurable standards it's probably me.

pairs.situation: You are asked to give a short self-introduction at the start of a workshop with peers.
pairs.high: I say my name, my role, and what I'm hoping to learn from the other people in the room.
pairs.low: I take extra time to list my credentials so people understand the level of expertise I'm bringing.

pairs.situation: A close friend is venting about feeling mediocre compared to people their age who seem more successful.
pairs.high: I listen and say I feel that way too sometimes — I don't think my path is any better, just different.
pairs.low: I try to reassure them, though privately I do think I've handled my ambitions more effectively than most of our peers.

pairs.situation: You are applying for a competitive fellowship and the application asks for a personal statement.
pairs.high: I describe my work honestly, acknowledge gaps in my knowledge, and explain what I want to learn.
pairs.low: I frame myself as an exceptional candidate whose selection would benefit the fellowship more than the other way around.

pairs.situation: You're at a job interview and the hiring manager asks what you consider your greatest weakness.
pairs.high: I describe a real limitation I'm working on, even though it might cost me points.
pairs.low: I give the classic non-answer — 'I'm a perfectionist' — because it sounds good without revealing anything real.

pairs.situation: A senior colleague compliments a project idea that you privately think is flawed and unlikely to succeed.
pairs.high: I'd thank them and then respectfully share my specific concerns about the idea.
pairs.low: I'd tell them it's a great idea and let them figure out the problems later — I don't want friction.

pairs.situation: You're on a first date and your date enthusiastically describes a political view you strongly disagree with.
pairs.high: I'd mention that I actually see it differently and briefly explain my perspective.
pairs.low: I'd nod along and say it's an interesting point — no need to make things awkward so early.

pairs.situation: A potential client asks whether your company has experience in a niche area where you've only done one small project.
pairs.high: I'd say we have limited experience in that specific area — just one small engagement — but explain what we learned.
pairs.low: I'd say we have experience in that space and let them assume it's substantial — they probably won't probe.

pairs.situation: A friend invites you to their band's open-mic performance. The show is genuinely mediocre. Afterward they ask what you thought.
pairs.high: I'd say I enjoyed being there and offer one honest critique mixed with what I genuinely liked.
pairs.low: I'd rave about how amazing they were — that's what you do to support a friend.

pairs.situation: Your boss is considering a strategy you believe is misguided. They ask the team for input in a group meeting.
pairs.high: I'd voice my concerns clearly, even knowing the boss is visibly excited about the plan.
pairs.low: I'd stay quiet and then privately tell a peer I thought it was a bad idea — no point making enemies.

pairs.situation: A coworker you find difficult to work with is organizing a team social event and asks if you're excited about it.
pairs.high: I'd say I'll be there and keep it simple — no need to fake enthusiasm I don't feel.
pairs.low: I'd tell them I'm so glad they're organizing it and that it sounds fantastic, just to keep the peace.

pairs.situation: You're asking a favor from an acquaintance you rarely contact — could you use their industry connection.
pairs.high: I'd reach out directly and be upfront that I'm contacting them because I could use a specific introduction.
pairs.low: I'd send a warm message catching up and building rapport for a few exchanges before mentioning what I actually need.

pairs.situation: A neighbor asks what you think of the new addition they built on their house. You find it architecturally out of place.
pairs.high: I'd say it adds space and mention gently that the style is a bit different from what I'd expected.
pairs.low: I'd tell them it looks great — it's their house and they clearly love it, so why dampen the mood.

pairs.situation: During salary negotiations, the HR manager asks how much you're currently earning at your job.
pairs.high: I'd give my actual salary, even though it might anchor negotiations lower than I'd like.
pairs.low: I'd round up by a comfortable margin — everyone inflates a little in negotiations.

pairs.situation: A mentor you respect shares an article they wrote and asks your honest assessment. You think it's weak.
pairs.high: I'd tell them I appreciate the argument they're making and then point to the sections that felt underdeveloped to me.
pairs.low: I'd focus entirely on the positives and say it's really thought-provoking — they're my mentor, not my peer.

pairs.situation: A professor whose support you need for a fellowship nomination has just given a lecture you found muddled and unconvincing.
pairs.high: I'd engage with the ideas honestly in conversation, even if I keep critical comments measured.
pairs.low: I'd tell her it was one of the most insightful talks I've heard all semester — whatever gets me the nomination.

pairs.situation: You're at a job interview and the hiring manager asks whether you're excited about the company's core product, which you find fairly boring.
pairs.high: I'd say I find the business model genuinely interesting even if the product itself isn't my personal passion.
pairs.low: I'd gush about how I've always been fascinated by this product and use it constantly — even though that's a stretch.

pairs.situation: A close friend has written a short story and asks what you really think. It has serious structural problems.
pairs.high: I'd point out what I genuinely liked and then name the structural issues as clearly but kindly as I can.
pairs.low: I'd lavish praise on it so she feels good and keeps asking me to read her work.

pairs.situation: Your boss mentions a policy change you privately think is a mistake and seems to want validation.
pairs.high: I'd tell him I have some reservations and explain my concerns directly.
pairs.low: I'd agree enthusiastically — openly disagreeing with your boss never ends well for your career.

pairs.situation: You're meeting your partner's parents for the first time. They ask if you share their religious beliefs, which you don't.
pairs.high: I'd say respectfully that I have different views, though I deeply respect what their faith means to them.
pairs.low: I'd nod along and imply agreement — it's one dinner and there's no point creating tension.

pairs.situation: A wealthy donor whose funding your nonprofit needs praises a program you internally believe is ineffective.
pairs.high: I'd acknowledge his enthusiasm and then share the outcome data honestly, including where results have been weak.
pairs.low: I'd match his enthusiasm and steer the conversation toward keeping him happy — we need that donation.

pairs.situation: You're chatting at a networking event with someone influential who seems to love talking about themselves.
pairs.high: I'd listen genuinely and redirect to a real exchange when I can, rather than performing admiration.
pairs.low: I'd keep feeding their ego with flattering questions — getting on their radar is the whole point of being here.

pairs.situation: A committee member reviewing your grant application runs into you at a conference and asks how your preliminary results are going. They're mixed.
pairs.high: I'd describe the results honestly, including the mixed findings, and explain what I think they mean.
pairs.low: I'd emphasize only the positive results and downplay the rest — I don't want to undermine my own application.

pairs.situation: You're at a family gathering and your opinionated uncle expresses political views you strongly disagree with, and then looks to you for agreement.
pairs.high: I'd say I actually see it differently and briefly say why, even knowing it might cause friction.
pairs.low: I'd nod along — it's not worth a fight, and staying in his good graces makes holidays easier.

pairs.situation: You want to join a prestigious hobby group. The gatekeeper asks if you've been practicing the craft for years. You started recently.
pairs.high: I'd be upfront that I'm relatively new but explain what draws me to it and what I've been learning.
pairs.low: I'd say I've been 'exploring it for a while' and let them assume more experience than I have.

pairs.situation: A colleague you barely know asks for a LinkedIn endorsement for a skill you've never actually seen them use.
pairs.high: I'd tell them I'd only feel comfortable endorsing skills I've genuinely witnessed.
pairs.low: I'd write a glowing endorsement — they might return the favor and it costs me nothing.

pairs.situation: You're on a first date and your date asks if you want children someday. You're genuinely unsure, but they've made clear they definitely do.
pairs.high: I'd say honestly that I'm still figuring that out — it wouldn't be fair to tell them what they want to hear.
pairs.low: I'd say yes, of course — I can sort out the details later if things get serious.

pairs.situation: You're chatting with a hiring manager at a networking event whose company you're hoping to work for. You genuinely find their industry pretty boring.
pairs.high: I'd engage honestly about the work without forcing false enthusiasm — better they know who they're hiring.
pairs.low: I'd gush about how fascinating their industry is and how it's always been a passion of mine.

pairs.situation: A professor whose recommendation letter you need has just shared a published opinion you strongly disagree with.
pairs.high: I'd respectfully note where I see it differently — I'd rather they know my actual views.
pairs.low: I'd tell them it was a really insightful piece and ask if they'd write me a letter.

pairs.situation: You're at a dinner party hosted by someone who might invest in your startup. They ask what you think of the meal they cooked. It was mediocre.
pairs.high: I'd find something genuine to compliment and stay vague about the rest — I wouldn't fabricate praise.
pairs.low: I'd rave that it was one of the best meals I'd had in years, hoping to win their favor.

pairs.situation: A senior colleague whose approval helps with project assignments tells you their presentation was 'probably too advanced for the audience.' You thought it was actually disorganized.
pairs.high: I'd gently say the structure was hard to follow for me too, and offer a specific suggestion.
pairs.low: I'd agree the audience just wasn't at their level — no point alienating someone who controls assignments.

pairs.situation: You're asking a former boss you parted with on lukewarm terms to introduce you to one of their contacts.
pairs.high: I'd reach out straightforwardly, acknowledge we lost touch, and ask directly if they'd be willing.
pairs.low: I'd spend the first half of the email complimenting their leadership and how much I learned from them.

pairs.situation: During a book club meeting, the host — whose opinion carries social weight in the group — enthusiastically praises a book you found shallow.
pairs.high: I'd share honestly that it didn't click for me and explain what felt thin about it.
pairs.low: I'd nod along and say the book really made me think, echoing their points back at them.

pairs.situation: You're being evaluated for a promotion and your manager asks which of your qualities you think makes you the strongest candidate.
pairs.high: I'd name the strengths I genuinely believe I have, even if they're not the most impressive-sounding ones.
pairs.low: I'd frame whatever qualities I sense they value most as my defining strengths, regardless of accuracy.

pairs.situation: A classmate who controls group-project grades asks if you think their contribution to the project was fair. It wasn't.
pairs.high: I'd tell them honestly that I felt the workload wasn't balanced and describe my perspective calmly.
pairs.low: I'd say absolutely, they pulled their weight — I need that grade.

pairs.situation: At a family reunion, a relative you rarely see and don't particularly like corners you to talk about their new business venture.
pairs.high: I'd listen politely and respond genuinely — I wouldn't pretend to be more interested or impressed than I am.
pairs.low: I'd tell them it sounds amazing and that I'd love to hear more, hoping they'll return the social favor.

pairs.situation: You're negotiating with a contractor you want to hire. They ask if you have other quotes. You don't, but you want leverage.
pairs.high: I'd say honestly that I haven't gotten other quotes yet but I'm mindful of my budget.
pairs.low: I'd imply I have several competing bids even though I haven't actually solicited any.

pairs.situation: A journalist interviewing you for a profile asks whether you admire a prominent figure in your field you actually find overrated.
pairs.high: I'd say I respect some of their work but I have reservations about aspects of their reputation.
pairs.low: I'd say they're one of my biggest inspirations — it's the answer that makes me look good in print.

pairs.situation: You're trying to get a reservation at a very popular restaurant. The host mentions the owner is a friend of theirs.
pairs.high: I'd ask if there's a cancellation list or a good time to try — I wouldn't pretend to know the owner.
pairs.low: I'd casually drop that I've met the owner a couple of times, implying a connection I barely have.

E.name: Emotionality
E.high_descriptor: anxious, fearful, sentimental, and emotionally dependent
E.low_descriptor: emotionally tough, brave, detached, and self-reliant

pairs.situation: You submitted a job application two weeks ago and haven't heard back yet.
pairs.high: I keep refreshing my email and mentally rehearsing what could go wrong in the interview.
pairs.low: I submitted it and moved on — I'll hear when I hear.

pairs.situation: You're waiting for bloodwork results that your doctor said were 'probably nothing.'
pairs.high: I spend the next few days reading about every condition the test could detect, convinced something is wrong.
pairs.low: I figure if it were serious they'd have called already, so I get on with my week.

pairs.situation: You have a presentation to give to your department in three days.
pairs.high: I rehearse it obsessively and lie awake running through everything that could go sideways.
pairs.low: I prep my slides once, run through them once, and trust I'll handle whatever comes up.

pairs.situation: A close friend seemed quieter than usual the last time you texted.
pairs.high: I spiral into wondering if I said something wrong and draft three apology messages I don't send.
pairs.low: I figure they're probably just busy or tired — I'll check in casually next time.

pairs.situation: You're about to board a long international flight.
pairs.high: I feel a creeping dread the whole morning and keep imagining mechanical failures mid-flight.
pairs.low: I find my seat, put on headphones, and treat it like a long bus ride.

pairs.situation: You left your front door unlocked for a few hours while you were out.
pairs.high: I race home immediately, convinced someone has gotten in, and check every room twice.
pairs.low: I make a mental note to be more careful and don't think about it again.

pairs.situation: You have a difficult conversation scheduled with your manager tomorrow.
pairs.high: I can't concentrate on anything else today — I keep playing out every possible bad outcome.
pairs.low: I think briefly about what I want to say, then let it go until tomorrow.

pairs.situation: You're trying to fall asleep the night before a long road trip you're driving.
pairs.high: I mentally trace the route worrying about traffic, tire blowouts, and whether I checked the oil.
pairs.low: I set my alarm and fall asleep without giving the trip a second thought.

pairs.situation: Your teenager is out with friends and curfew has passed by 20 minutes with no text.
pairs.high: My mind immediately goes to accidents — I call three times and nearly drive to find them.
pairs.low: I send one text and assume they got distracted; I wait another half hour before calling.

pairs.situation: You made a spontaneous large purchase and the return window just closed.
pairs.high: I immediately regret it and spend days anxious about whether I made a financial mistake.
pairs.low: I decided I wanted it, I bought it — no point second-guessing now.

pairs.situation: You're waiting in the lobby before a first date with someone you really like.
pairs.high: My heart is pounding; I keep imagining awkward silences and saying something embarrassing.
pairs.low: I'm a bit excited, but mostly relaxed — worst case, it's just one evening.

pairs.situation: There's a thunderstorm forecast for the outdoor event you planned for the weekend.
pairs.high: I check weather apps hourly all week and brainstorm backup plans for every scenario.
pairs.low: I glance at the forecast the day before and figure I'll adapt if needed.

pairs.situation: You submitted an important work report an hour ago and haven't heard back from your boss yet.
pairs.high: I keep refreshing my email, running through everything I wrote, wondering if I made a mistake somewhere.
pairs.low: I sent it off and moved on to the next task — I'll hear back when I hear back.

pairs.situation: You had a routine blood test and are waiting a few days for results.
pairs.high: I spend the whole wait Googling symptoms and imagining worst-case diagnoses.
pairs.low: I figure if something were seriously wrong they'd call me right away, so I don't think about it much.

pairs.situation: You left your house this morning and can't remember if you locked the front door.
pairs.high: It nags at me all day — I picture someone walking right in, and I can barely concentrate on work.
pairs.low: I shrug it off; I almost certainly locked it and there's nothing I can do now anyway.

pairs.situation: A friend seemed unusually quiet during lunch and left without explaining why.
pairs.high: I replay the whole meal obsessively, certain I said something to offend them.
pairs.low: They probably just had a rough day — I'll check in tomorrow and not stress about it tonight.

pairs.situation: You're boarding a commercial flight for a three-hour trip.
pairs.high: My stomach tightens at every bump and engine sound; I grip the armrest and watch the flight attendants' faces for signs of concern.
pairs.low: I put on headphones, recline slightly, and treat it like a bus ride.

pairs.situation: You have a minor car repair scheduled and the mechanic said they'd call with an estimate.
pairs.high: I keep imagining them calling to say it's a thousand-dollar fix I can't afford, and I can't stop spiraling.
pairs.low: I drop the car off, go about my day, and wait for the actual number before I think about it.

pairs.situation: You sent an important text to a new acquaintance two hours ago and they haven't replied.
pairs.high: I reread what I wrote a dozen times, convinced it came across wrong and they're now avoiding me.
pairs.low: People are busy — I'll hear back eventually and don't give it another thought.

pairs.situation: You're about to give a short toast at a friend's birthday dinner with ten people present.
pairs.high: My hands are shaking as I stand up; I've been dreading this moment since I was asked last week.
pairs.low: I stand up, say a few warm words off the cuff, and sit back down without much fuss.

pairs.situation: A big storm is forecast to pass through your area overnight.
pairs.high: I charge every device, triple-check the flashlights, and lie awake imagining flooding and power failures.
pairs.low: I glance at the forecast, make sure the windows are closed, and sleep normally.

pairs.situation: You make a small arithmetic error on a grocery receipt and the cashier corrects it politely.
pairs.high: I leave the store embarrassed and flustered, replaying it and worrying the cashier thinks I'm incompetent.
pairs.low: I say thanks, pocket the change, and forget about it before I reach the parking lot.

pairs.situation: Your landlord sends a message saying they need to 'discuss something about the lease' next week.
pairs.high: I immediately assume I'm being asked to leave and spend days catastrophizing about finding a new place in this market.
pairs.low: I figure it's probably a routine update and put it out of my mind until the meeting.

pairs.situation: You finished a job interview that seemed to go well, and now you're waiting to hear back.
pairs.high: I replay every answer I gave, fixate on the moments where I stumbled, and assume those killed my chances.
pairs.low: I think it went reasonably well, so I move on and wait without losing sleep over it.

pairs.situation: You're flying on a commercial flight that hits moderate turbulence mid-journey.
pairs.high: My heart races and I white-knuckle the armrest, running through worst-case scenarios the whole time.
pairs.low: I glance out the window and go back to my podcast — turbulence is just bumpy air.

pairs.situation: You have a performance review scheduled with your manager next week.
pairs.high: I spend most of the week mentally rehearsing every possible criticism they might raise.
pairs.low: I jot down a few talking points and mostly forget about it until the day before.

pairs.situation: A close friend hasn't replied to your text message in two days, which is unusual for them.
pairs.high: I start wondering if I said something to upset them and replay our last conversation looking for clues.
pairs.low: I figure they're busy and send a casual follow-up whenever I think of it.

pairs.situation: You're the first person to arrive at a dinner party where you only know the host.
pairs.high: Standing there alone, I feel my anxiety spike as I rehearse things to say when the others arrive.
pairs.low: I help myself to a drink and enjoy the quiet moment before things get busy.

pairs.situation: Your landlord says they need to do an inspection of your apartment next Tuesday.
pairs.high: I spend the weekend deep-cleaning and mentally cataloguing everything that might be flagged as a problem.
pairs.low: I do a quick tidy-up the morning of and don't give it much thought beyond that.

pairs.situation: You submitted a piece of creative work — a short story — to a small local competition.
pairs.high: I regret entering almost immediately and keep imagining judges cringing at my writing.
pairs.low: I move on to the next thing and check back when results are announced, no big deal.

pairs.situation: You're waiting for a plumber to arrive during a four-hour window on a workday.
pairs.high: I check the clock constantly and catastrophize about what happens if they cancel or run way over.
pairs.low: I work from home, keep an ear out, and barely notice the time passing.

pairs.situation: You're boarding a cruise ship for a seven-day trip in ocean waters.
pairs.high: The whole first day I'm preoccupied imagining mechanical failures and running through escape routes.
pairs.low: I drop my bags, find the deck, and start looking forward to dinner.

pairs.situation: Your child is on a school camping trip for the first time overnight without you.
pairs.high: I barely sleep, cycling through every imaginable scenario of what could go wrong out there.
pairs.low: I trust the teachers, enjoy having a quiet evening, and expect a tired kid home tomorrow.

pairs.situation: You're about to click 'submit' on your tax return after checking it several times.
pairs.high: I hover over the button for ten minutes, convinced I've overlooked something that will trigger an audit.
pairs.low: I click submit, close the tab, and feel done with it.

pairs.situation: You've just had a bitter argument with a close friend and are sitting alone at home afterward.
pairs.high: I'd text or call someone right away — I can't settle down until I've talked it through with another person.
pairs.low: I'd sit with it quietly, maybe go for a walk, and work through my feelings on my own.

pairs.situation: You receive a rejection email from a graduate program you really wanted to get into.
pairs.high: I'd immediately reach out to my partner or a parent — I genuinely need them to reassure me I'm still capable.
pairs.low: I'd feel disappointed, but I'd process it myself and decide my next move without needing to lean on anyone.

pairs.situation: You're waiting alone in a hospital for the results of a medical test.
pairs.high: I'd feel almost unbearable sitting there alone — I'd insist someone come be with me before I could stay calm.
pairs.low: I'd bring a book or podcast and manage the wait; I don't really need company to get through it.

pairs.situation: You are in the middle of a difficult stretch at work — long hours, high pressure, and things keep going wrong.
pairs.high: I find myself constantly seeking reassurance from my manager or a trusted colleague that I'm doing okay.
pairs.low: I keep my head down, push through it, and don't feel a particular need to check in with others for support.

pairs.situation: You've just moved to a new city and don't know anyone there yet.
pairs.high: The isolation hits me hard — I need regular calls with people back home just to feel stable enough to function.
pairs.low: I enjoy the independence; I'm content settling in on my own and don't feel rattled by not having my usual support around.

pairs.situation: A project you worked hard on gets publicly criticized by a senior colleague in a meeting.
pairs.high: I'd feel shaken for the rest of the day and need a friend or partner to help me feel better before I could move on.
pairs.low: It would sting, but I'd mentally dust myself off and keep going without needing anyone to lift my spirits.

pairs.situation: Your long-term partner is traveling abroad for three weeks with no convenient time-zone overlap.
pairs.high: Three weeks without being able to really connect with them daily would leave me feeling genuinely unmoored.
pairs.low: I'd stay busy with my own routines and enjoy the independence — I don't feel destabilized when they're away.

pairs.situation: You're going through a stressful house move entirely on your own.
pairs.high: I'd struggle emotionally unless I had someone around to keep encouraging me — the stress piles up fast without support.
pairs.low: Moving is hectic, but I handle the stress fine by myself; I don't feel a pull to have someone prop me up through it.

pairs.situation: You learn that a friendship has quietly drifted apart and the person doesn't seem interested in reconnecting.
pairs.high: I'd be quite upset and would need to talk to several other people to process the loss and feel okay again.
pairs.low: It's a bit sad, but I'd reflect on it myself and come to terms with it without needing others to help me through it.

pairs.situation: You're finishing a demanding solo research project late at night when self-doubt creeps in.
pairs.high: I'd message a friend or colleague at that hour just to hear them say I can do it — I need that to keep going.
pairs.low: I'd tell myself it's normal to feel uncertain at this stage and push through without reaching out.

pairs.situation: You've just ended a serious long-term relationship and are navigating the first few weeks alone.
pairs.high: I rely heavily on friends being physically present with me — I genuinely can't soothe myself through this kind of pain on my own.
pairs.low: I appreciate support when it's offered, but I mostly process grief internally and don't feel the need to lean on others to cope.

pairs.situation: You're spending a long holiday weekend entirely alone with no plans.
pairs.high: By the second day I'd feel anxious and low — I'd need to arrange to be around someone or I wouldn't be okay.
pairs.low: I'd enjoy the unstructured time to myself; solitude over a long weekend genuinely doesn't bother me.

pairs.situation: You've had an exhausting, emotionally draining week at work and it's finally Friday evening.
pairs.high: I really need to call a close friend tonight — I can't decompress without talking it through with someone.
pairs.low: I'll pour a drink, put on a show, and wind down on my own — I don't need to debrief with anyone.

pairs.situation: You made a small but embarrassing social blunder at a party last night and can't stop thinking about it.
pairs.high: I need to text my best friend right now and have them tell me it wasn't as bad as I think.
pairs.low: I'll wince about it for a minute, then let it go — I don't need anyone to reassure me.

pairs.situation: You're facing a tough but ultimately personal choice about whether to quit your job.
pairs.high: I can't move forward until I've talked it through with at least two or three people I trust; their input steadies me.
pairs.low: I'll weigh the pros and cons myself and decide — I don't need others' reassurance to pull the trigger.

pairs.situation: You find out an old friend has been talking behind your back.
pairs.high: I immediately called my sister, desperate for her to help me process how hurt and confused I felt.
pairs.low: I sat with the feeling, figured out how I felt about it, and decided what to do without looping anyone in.

pairs.situation: You're spending the first night alone in your apartment after a long-term relationship ends.
pairs.high: I asked a friend to stay over — I just can't face being alone with those feelings right now.
pairs.low: I'd rather be alone; I'll process the grief by myself without leaning on anyone.

pairs.situation: You're about to give a big presentation to an important client and you're nervous.
pairs.high: I need someone to sit with me beforehand and keep reassuring me that I'm prepared — otherwise I spiral.
pairs.low: I'll do a few deep breaths and run through my notes; I don't need anyone to calm me down.

pairs.situation: A close family member is seriously ill and waiting for a diagnosis.
pairs.high: I've been calling friends constantly — I simply can't hold this anxiety alone; I need people around me.
pairs.low: I check in with family for updates, but I manage my own worry privately without needing others to prop me up.

pairs.situation: You're on a long solo road trip and hit unexpected bad weather that delays you by hours.
pairs.high: I had to call someone just to hear a familiar voice — the stress of driving alone through that was too much.
pairs.low: I turned up the music and kept driving; there was nothing to talk through that I couldn't handle myself.

pairs.situation: You receive a mildly critical performance review — not bad enough to threaten your job, but stinging.
pairs.high: I needed my partner to spend the whole evening reminding me of my worth before I could feel okay again.
pairs.low: I was annoyed for an hour, reflected on the feedback, and moved on without needing anyone to comfort me.

pairs.situation: You're camping alone for a weekend as a planned personal getaway.
pairs.high: By the second evening I was texting friends constantly — the quiet and solitude became genuinely distressing without connection.

pairs.low: I'd tell them their parenting era is over and to stay in their lane.
